methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $5M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
DIRAC is a seed-stage robotics and manufacturing company founded by Fil Aronshtein that has raised $5M+. The sector peer universe comprises nine private companies and one public company, though actionable comparables at an equivalent stage and valuation tier remain limited.

Stage-progression analysis is the primary applicable method given data constraints. Using total capital raised ($5M) as a proxy for seed-stage valuation and applying a 2.5x step-up multiplier typical of seed-to-next-stage advancement yields $12.5M. This method is mechanically sound but assumes DIRAC's exit velocity aligns with sector norms. A peer-median approach could not be applied: while seven robotics and manufacturing companies exist in the broader universe, none satisfy minimum thresholds for same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-valuation-tier clustering. Falling back to all-sector medians would introduce material noise.

The triangulated range is $11M to $14M, with a median of $12M, derived entirely from the stage-progression method. This narrow methodological foundation introduces meaningful uncertainty. Key variables include: (1) whether the $5M+ proxy accurately reflects valuation consensus at this stage; (2) execution risk and product-market fit relative to the peer cohort; and (3) sector-specific growth rates in robotics manufacturing, which may diverge from the 2.5x multiplier baseline. Expansion to adjacent stages or relaxed peer filtering criteria could narrow confidence intervals but risks introducing cross-stage bias. Further diligence on comparable seed-stage closings in robotics would be required to validate this range.
